articles.title: District of Columbia home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI
articles.dek: Quarterly, annual, and longer-run state HPI comparisons with a clear boundary between market movement and property value.
articles.previewText: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for District of Columbia and why an index cannot value a specific property.
articles.metaDescription: District of Columbia home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI. Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for District of Columbia and why an index cannot.

sections.heading: The latest statewide index reading
body: District of Columbia's latest FHFA purchase-only HPI is 610.4, accompanied by a quarterly change of 0.1%. For District of Columbia, the 610.4 index level is a benchmark for repeat-sale price movement, not a dollar-denominated typical home price. A quarter in which the index rose by 0.1% can help a borrower date the statewide market context, but it cannot identify the value or asking price of a selected property. Reading 610.4 with 0.1% also keeps the level separate from the rate of change. Alongside the 610.4 state index, local inventory, property type, condition, concessions, taxes, insurance, and financing costs still need current evidence before the trend informs a decision.

sections.heading: Quarterly and annual windows tell different stories
body: Over the latest quarter, District of Columbia's purchase-only HPI rose by 0.1%; over the latest year, it fell by -1.4%. The 0.1% figure is more sensitive to recent movement, while -1.4% compares the latest quarter with the matching quarter a year earlier. A borrower should not choose between the 0.1% and -1.4% windows based only on which looks more favorable, because the comparisons answer different timing questions. Together they show whether recent statewide movement is aligned with or different from the broader year without predicting the next quarter, a local bidding environment, or the price path of one home in District of Columbia.

sections.heading: Place the latest movement in a longer sequence
body: Across the five-year comparison, the District of Columbia HPI rose by 1.1%, compared with the latest annual movement of -1.4%. The 1.1% change places the current 610.4 index reading in a longer repeat-sale sequence, but it can span very different rate, inventory, and economic conditions. The 1.1% change should not be applied to an old purchase price to estimate a current property value. For a borrower, the contrast between 1.1% and -1.4% is most useful as a reminder that search assumptions can age at different speeds. Even when the 1.1% direction is clear, current comparable properties and a property-specific payment worksheet remain necessary.

sections.heading: Use the index to test local assumptions
body: The combination of a 0.1% quarterly change, -1.4% annual change, and 1.1% five-year change gives District of Columbia borrowers three dated views of the same statewide index. If the 0.1%, -1.4%, and 1.1% windows point in different directions, the divergence shows that timing matters; if they point together, statewide consistency still does not erase local variation. A District of Columbia search plan can use those three changes to ask whether current listings, seller concessions, and property types resemble the broad state movement. It should not use 610.4 as a sale price or use any of the percentage changes as a forecast, because FHFA HPI measures repeat-sale movement rather than the complete set of homes available to one borrower.

sections.heading: The HPI cannot value a property or set a payment
body: An HPI level of 610.4 does not establish a District of Columbia property's dollar value, and an -1.4% annual movement does not establish how much any home gained or lost. The 610.4 index excludes the borrower's down payment, loan amount, interest rate, taxes, insurance, association costs, repairs, and time horizon. FHFA can also revise published values, so the 0.1% and 1.1% comparisons should retain their release period. The 610.4 index level must remain separate from the dollar figures used in a property review. Those limits make the 610.4 state index useful for orientation but unsuitable as an appraisal, offer recommendation, equity calculation, promised appreciation claim, payment quote, or underwriting conclusion.

sections.heading: Connect the state trend to current property evidence
body: A practical District of Columbia borrower review can record 0.1%, -1.4%, and 1.1% as statewide trend markers, then compare them with current local listings and recent property-level evidence. For a purchase, keep the 0.1% state change as context while adding the selected price, inspection findings, appraisal process, taxes, insurance, cash-to-close, and loan disclosures. For a refinance or equity question, begin with a defensible property review rather than multiplying a prior value by -1.4% or 1.1%. This approach preserves the information in the 610.4 HPI while giving current local and borrower-specific facts the decision-making role that a statewide repeat-sale index cannot fill.

articles.methodology: This article uses the official FHFA purchase-only state quarterly HPI and calculates changes from matching prior-quarter, prior-year, and five-year index observations when available. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: FHFA may revise index values, statewide movement can mask local and property-type differences, and an index level is not a dollar-denominated home value. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: District of Columbia statewide labor market: latest employment evidence
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest BLS labor-force, employment, unemployment, and rate measures for District of Columbia.
articles.previewText: See what changed in District of Columbia's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
articles.metaDescription: District of Columbia statewide labor market: latest employment evidence. See what changed in District of Columbia's labor market and why broad employment data.

evidenceFacts.label: change in unemployment rate from the prior month
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier

sections.heading: The latest labor-force counts
body: The latest LAUS release reports a District of Columbia labor force of 403,744, with 378,936 people employed and 24,808 unemployed under the program's definitions. Those three counts belong together: 378,936 plus 24,808 explains the scale behind the published 6.1% unemployment rate. For mortgage planning, the 403,744 state-level labor force describes the surrounding market rather than the income or employment record of an applicant. A borrower can use the 403,744 labor-force baseline to understand the size of the measured market, but qualification still depends on documented income, employment continuity, debts, assets, and the rules of the loan being considered.

sections.heading: What changed from the prior month
body: District of Columbia's unemployment rate fell from 6.2% in the prior month to 6.1% in the latest month, a change of -0.1%. A -0.1% move can reflect current conditions, normal sampling movement, and later revisions, so it should be read with the underlying 24,808 unemployment count and 403,744 labor force. The -0.1% comparison does not show that a particular employer is hiring or cutting jobs, and it cannot predict the next release. For a District of Columbia borrower, the monthly change is a prompt to date economic assumptions and verify personal employment documentation, not a reason to assume approval, denial, or a particular loan price.

sections.heading: The year-over-year comparison
body: Compared with 6.1% a year earlier, the latest District of Columbia unemployment rate of 6.1% was unchanged by 0.0%. The year-over-year window reduces the risk of interpreting only one monthly step, but the 0.0% difference still describes a population estimate rather than an individual's job stability. Reading 6.1%, 6.2%, and 6.1% together shows whether the latest month sits within a wider movement without forecasting what follows. A borrower should keep that broad District of Columbia pattern separate from pay history, variable compensation, self-employment records, contracts, leave, job changes, and other facts a lender may need to document.

sections.heading: Why the rate needs its underlying counts
body: At 6.1%, District of Columbia's unemployment rate summarizes 24,808 unemployed people within a 403,744 labor force; the rate should not be discussed without those counts. The 378,936 employment estimate is an economic measure and does not distinguish borrowers by hours, tenure, pay structure, industry, or the documentation available for a loan file. That makes 378,936 useful for local context but unsuitable as evidence that one household's income is stable or sufficient. Keeping the 6.1% rate attached to 403,744, 378,936, and 24,808 prevents a small percentage movement from carrying more borrower-level meaning than the LAUS release supports.

sections.heading: Labor statistics are not an income review
body: Neither 378,936 employed people nor a 6.1% unemployment rate tells a lender what one District of Columbia applicant earns, whether that income is eligible, or whether it is likely to continue. The -0.1% monthly rate change also cannot substitute for pay stubs, tax returns, business records, contracts, benefit documentation, or an explanation of a recent job transition. The 6.1% rate can be relevant to timing and local economic questions while remaining outside the underwriting decision. That boundary protects borrowers from treating a favorable 0.0% year-over-year change as an approval signal or an unfavorable movement as a conclusion about their own documented capacity.

sections.heading: Turn the trend into documentation questions
body: A practical next step for a District of Columbia borrower is to note the latest 6.1% unemployment rate and the 0.0% year-over-year change, then move immediately to the records that describe the household. After noting the 6.1% rate, compare current base pay, variable earnings, self-employment history, contracts, assets, debts, and expected changes with the documentation requirements for the loan path. The 403,744 local labor force and 378,936 employment count can frame the economic conversation, but they should not alter verified borrower inputs. The -0.1% monthly movement remains a population measure even when it appears consistent with a borrower's personal experience. A licensed professional can explain which documents and time periods matter while keeping District of Columbia's 6.1% LAUS rate in its proper role as dated local economic context.

articles.methodology: This article uses BLS LAUS state monthly series for labor force, employment, unemployment, and unemployment rate, with prior-month and year-ago comparisons. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: LAUS estimates can be revised, city series are unadjusted, and local labor statistics describe a population rather than an individual applicant's income or employment. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

relatedRoutes.label: District of Columbia mortgage and housing guide

articles.title: District of Columbia housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS
articles.dek: Statewide evidence on home value, income, owner costs, housing stock, tenure, vacancy, and rent for borrower planning.
articles.previewText: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for District of Columbia with survey limitations and practical questions.
articles.metaDescription: District of Columbia housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS. Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for District of.
keyTakeaways: prior ACS state median home value: $601,500.
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage: $3,128.

evidenceFacts.label: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage
change from the prior ACS home-value estimate

sections.heading: Statewide value, income, and owner costs
body: District of Columbia's 2024 ACS housing baseline reports a median owner-occupied home value of $737,100, median household income of $109,870, and median selected monthly owner costs of $3,128 for owners with a mortgage. The $737,100, $109,870, and $3,128 values are separate statewide medians, so they should not be treated as the price, income, and monthly budget of one typical household. The $3,128 measure combines recurring ownership expenses and is not a real-estate-tax figure or a current mortgage quote. For planning, $737,100, $109,870, and $3,128 identify statewide reference points and cost categories, while a real decision still requires local property, borrower, tax, insurance, and loan information.

sections.heading: Keep owner costs and rent comparable
body: The same District of Columbia release reports $1,954 in median gross rent alongside $3,128 in median selected monthly owner costs for owners with a mortgage. The two measures cover different housing populations and cost definitions, so $1,954 should not be compared with only principal and interest or used to declare a statewide break-even point. A useful comparison replaces the $1,954 and $3,128 medians with a current lease and a current property budget, including utilities, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, transaction costs, and the expected time horizon. The $109,870 statewide median can provide economic context, but dividing it by either $1,954 or $3,128 would still combine medians that do not represent the same household.

sections.heading: How occupied housing is divided by tenure
body: Across District of Columbia's measured housing stock of 361,014 units, the owner share of occupied housing is 41.5% and the renter share is 58.5%, making renter-occupied housing the larger statewide tenure group. The 41.5% and 58.5% shares describe how occupied units were distributed during the survey period; they do not measure current buyer demand, owner willingness to sell, or renter readiness to purchase. The 41.5% and 58.5% statewide tenure split is useful for understanding scale and checking assumptions about the housing mix. It remains too broad to select a product or characterize a particular city, county, property type, or household inside District of Columbia.

sections.heading: Vacancy is broader than homes for sale
body: District of Columbia's vacant-unit share is 10.1% within a statewide stock of 361,014 housing units. ACS vacancy includes categories beyond property currently offered for sale or rent, so 10.1% is not a live inventory rate and cannot measure competition for a selected home. Read beside the 41.5% owner share and 58.5% renter share, the vacancy figure helps describe how the broad stock was classified without predicting near-term supply. Borrowers should use current listings, property condition, days on market, concessions, and local professional context to answer transaction questions that 10.1% and 361,014 were not designed to resolve.

sections.heading: The statewide home-value estimate changed across periods
body: District of Columbia's median home-value estimate increased from $601,500 in the prior ACS period to $737,100 in the current release, a reported change of 22.5%. Because both estimates summarize multi-year survey windows, 22.5% is not a point-to-point appreciation rate for a property purchased at $601,500. The 22.5% comparison can show that an old statewide reference may need updating, but it cannot explain the value path in each local market or price segment. Margins of error, changes in the housing mix, and current property evidence should be reviewed before the move from $601,500 to $737,100 influences a purchase, refinance, or equity assumption.

sections.heading: Move from statewide context to a local worksheet
body: A borrower can carry $737,100, $3,128, $1,954, and the 10.1% vacancy share into a local comparison as dated statewide context, then replace each with evidence matched to the actual decision. For ownership, replace the $737,100 and $3,128 medians with a selected price, down payment, current loan disclosures, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, and cash reserves. For renting, replace the $1,954 median with a comparable lease, utilities, deposits, and expected changes over the same horizon. The 58.5% renter share is useful for describing the statewide tenure mix, not for predicting which local option will be available. The 41.5% tenure measure can help explain the statewide housing mix, but it cannot decide whether buying, renting, refinancing, or using equity is suitable for one District of Columbia household.

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 ACS 5-year state estimates for home value, household income, selected owner costs, rent, total and occupied housing units, vacancy, and owner and renter tenure, plus a 2019 value comparison. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: ACS estimates cover multi-year survey periods, carry margins of error, and do not measure current listings, current financing terms, or a specific household's housing costs. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: District of Columbia 2026 county loan-limit landscape
articles.dek: How conforming and FHA one-unit limits vary across District of Columbia counties and how borrowers can use them responsibly.
articles.previewText: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across District of Columbia without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
articles.metaDescription: District of Columbia 2026 county loan-limit landscape. See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across District of Columbia without treating a.
sourceLabels: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
keyTakeaways: counties in the statewide limit summary: 1.

evidenceFacts.label: counties in the statewide limit summary

sections.heading: The conforming range across county records
body: District of Columbia's statewide summary covers 1 county or county-equivalent records. Within those records, the 2026 one-unit conforming limit runs from $1,249,125 to $1,249,125. The matched records share a one-unit conforming figure of $1,249,125, but the applicable county row still must be verified for a property. The 1 record count keeps the range tied to the statewide source coverage rather than an assumed national rule. The $1,249,125 to $1,249,125 amounts classify loan size within the published program table; they do not establish a borrower's capacity, a property's value, or the price of financing. Before using the $1,249,125 statewide maximum, county, legal unit count, and expected base loan amount must be known.

sections.heading: The FHA range follows its own county table
body: Across the same 1 District of Columbia records, the 2026 one-unit FHA limit spans $1,249,125 to $1,249,125. The matched FHA rows share $1,249,125, although property geography and current program guidance still require confirmation. The $1,249,125 floor and $1,249,125 high remain county-table values even when they match conforming amounts elsewhere. The FHA range should not be merged with the $1,249,125 to $1,249,125 conforming range, because the programs use different authorities and requirements. A $1,249,125 to $1,249,125 comparison can route loan-amount and product-category questions, but it cannot determine FHA eligibility, mortgage insurance, appraisal acceptability, occupancy compliance, cash needs, or approval.

sections.heading: Why county verification matters
body: 0 District of Columbia counties sit above the conforming baseline in this summary, while the full conforming range reaches from $1,249,125 to $1,249,125. That count shows where a statewide shortcut can fail: a property in a higher-limit county may use a different published ceiling than one at $1,249,125. The 0 figure does not mean those counties are more affordable, more desirable, or more likely to approve a loan. It only describes how many matched county rows exceed the baseline. The $1,249,125 maximum should therefore be treated as evidence that county lookup matters, not as the default District of Columbia limit. Before treating $1,249,125 as relevant, a borrower should identify the property's official county or county-equivalent area and then attach conventional, jumbo, or FHA labels to the desired loan amount.

sections.heading: Conforming and FHA ranges answer different questions
body: The highest one-unit conforming amount in District of Columbia is $1,249,125, while the highest FHA amount is $1,249,125; at the low end, the figures are $1,249,125 and $1,249,125. These pairs show program boundaries, not competing offers. A requested amount below $1,249,125 may still fall outside the applicable county's conforming limit, and an amount below $1,249,125 may still face FHA borrower and property requirements. Comparing $1,249,125, $1,249,125, $1,249,125, and $1,249,125 is useful only when county and unit count are held constant. The $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 ceilings leave rates, fees, mortgage insurance, appraisal, reserves, debt capacity, and product suitability as separate questions for the documented transaction.

sections.heading: A larger county limit is not greater affordability
body: A $1,249,125 conforming ceiling or $1,249,125 FHA ceiling does not make that loan amount affordable to a District of Columbia household, and the lower $1,249,125 or $1,249,125 figures do not describe home prices. Published $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 limits cannot establish borrower income, debts, credit, assets, occupancy, property condition, appraisal, insurance, reserves, rates, fees, or final approval. The $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 figures can also change with a new calendar year or corrected property geography. Keeping the 1 county-record scope and 2026 period attached to the figures prevents the statewide summary from being mistaken for a personalized product recommendation or a single amount that applies everywhere.

sections.heading: Use the range to reach the correct county row
body: The next step is to verify the selected property's District of Columbia county or county-equivalent area, legal unit count, price, down-payment plan, and expected base loan amount. Then compare that amount with the exact official row, using the $1,249,125 to $1,249,125 conforming range and $1,249,125 to $1,249,125 FHA range only as orientation. The 0 above-baseline count explains why this lookup matters, but it does not replace it. Keep the selected county row with the worksheet so the $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 statewide maximums cannot be mistaken for property-specific figures. After the 0 count prompts that county lookup, a licensed professional can explain current limits and borrower, property, mortgage-insurance, and documentation rules without presenting the largest statewide figure as available borrowing power.

articles.methodology: This article joins official 2026 FHFA conforming and HUD FHA county files by five-digit county FIPS, then summarizes the observed one-unit range and county counts for the state. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: State summaries can conceal special exceptions and multi-unit differences, and official program updates or property-location corrections can change the applicable row. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.
